attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the labour ward at 41 weeks gestation for induction of labour. She has a history of one previous caesarean section three years ago for failure to progress. Her current pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals a term pregnancy with cephalic presentation. Vaginal examination shows a closed cervix, 1 cm long, and the fetal head at station -2. Cardiotocography shows a reassuring fetal heart rate pattern.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate method for induction of labour in this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting for induction of labour with a history of one previous caesarean section, making this a case of attempted vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC). The choice of induction method is crucial in VBAC cases due to the increased risk of uterine rupture.

The cervical ripening balloon is the most appropriate method for induction of labour in this scenario. Mechanical methods of induction, such as the cervical ripening balloon, are preferred in VBAC attempts because they carry a lower risk of uterine rupture compared to pharmacological methods. The cervical ripening balloon catheter works by applying pressure to the cervix, stimulating the release of endogenous prostaglandins and promoting cervical ripening.

Intravaginal prostaglandin E2 gel is contraindicated in women with a previous caesarean section due to the increased risk of uterine rupture. Prostaglandins can cause excessive uterine contractions, potentially leading to separation of the previous caesarean scar.

Oral misoprostol, a prostaglandin E1 analogue, is also contraindicated in VBAC attempts for the same reasons as prostaglandin E2. The risk of uterine rupture is significantly higher with misoprostol compared to other methods of induction in women with a scarred uterus.

Intravenous oxytocin infusion can be used cautiously in VBAC inductions, but it is not the first-line choice, especially when the cervix is unfavourable (as in this case with a closed, 1 cm long cervix). Oxytocin is typically reserved for augmentation of labour once it has started or after cervical ripening has been achieved.

Artificial rupture of membranes alone is not appropriate as the primary method of induction in this case, given the unfavourable cervix. It is more commonly used in conjunction with other methods or when labour has already started.

In managing VBAC inductions, it's crucial to have a thorough discussion with the patient about the risks and benefits, obtain informed consent, and ensure close monitoring throughout the induction and labour process. The choice of induction method should always be individualised based on the patient's specific circumstances and local guidelines.
learningPoints: Mechanical methods of induction, such as the cervical ripening balloon catheter, are preferred for women attempting vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) due to lower risk of uterine rupture.
Prostaglandins are generally contraindicated in VBAC attempts due to increased risk of uterine rupture.
Careful monitoring and informed consent are crucial in managing labour induction for VBAC patients.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Operative Vaginal Delivery. Green-top Guideline No. 45 [Internet]. 2011 [cited 2024 Oct 9]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/media/kpkjwd5h/gtg_45.pdf
